title: Stroke FAST and last known well record
category: Medical and first · Aid · Disaster · Health
summary: Medical-heavy survival entry for face-arm-speech-time observations and the exact last-known-well time. It is designed for offline emergencies and does not replace professional care.
content: Use this entry to organize face-arm-speech-time observations and the exact last-known-well time. Start with scene safety, hand hygiene, calm communication, and written timing. Keep the person comfortable, protect privacy, avoid risky experiments, and prepare a clear handover for a doctor, ambulance, clinic, pharmacist, or trained first-aider. Watch especially for: face droop, arm weakness, speech trouble, sudden vision loss, severe headache, or imbalance. In a long emergency, review the person regularly instead of assuming they are stable.
materials: clean water if available. Context: “Stroke FAST and last known well record”.
soap or sanitizer if available — for «Stroke FAST and last known well record»
gloves or clean barrier if available — for «Stroke FAST and last known well record»
paper and pen. Context: “Stroke FAST and last known well record”.
phone or radio if available — for «Stroke FAST and last known well record»
labels for times and medicines — for «Stroke FAST and last known well record»
steps: Check that the place is safe for the helper and the injured or sick person. Apply it specifically within the “Stroke FAST and last known well record” procedure.
Call emergency help or send a reliable person for help when any red flag is present. Apply it specifically within the “Stroke FAST and last known well record” procedure.
Write the time the problem started, what changed, medicines taken, allergies, and known conditions. Apply it specifically within the “Stroke FAST and last known well record” procedure.

Use clean hands and clean barriers before touching wounds, body fluids, food, medicines, infants, or medical devices. Apply it specifically within the “Stroke FAST and last known well record” procedure.
Keep the person warm or cool as appropriate, still if trauma is possible, and protected from crowds. Apply it specifically within the “Stroke FAST and last known well record” procedure.
Recheck and record changes regularly so deterioration is noticed early. Apply it specifically within the “Stroke FAST and last known well record” procedure.
Prepare a short handover note with name, age, symptoms, times, medicines, allergies, and actions already taken. Apply it specifically within the “Stroke FAST and last known well record” procedure.
warnings: Do not use this as a replacement for training, emergency services, a clinician, or local medical guidance. Treat this as a specific boundary while managing “Stroke FAST and last known well record”.

Do not guess medicine doses, mix medicines without label checking, share prescriptions, or perform invasive procedures. Treat this as a specific boundary while managing “Stroke FAST and last known well record”.
Escalate urgently if you see: face droop, arm weakness, speech trouble, sudden vision loss, severe headache, or imbalance.
Keep children, elders, pregnancy, disability, immune weakness, and chronic illness in the high-risk group. Treat this as a specific boundary while managing “Stroke FAST and last known well record”.
common_mistakes: Waiting too long after red flags appear while carrying out “Stroke FAST and last known well record”
Not writing times and medicines while carrying out “Stroke FAST and last known well record”
Touching wounds or fluids without hand hygiene while carrying out “Stroke FAST and last known well record”
Letting crowds block air, privacy, or transport while carrying out “Stroke FAST and last known well record”
Assuming a quiet person is stable without rechecking while carrying out “Stroke FAST and last known well record”
alternatives: Use official first-aid training when available as a fallback for “Stroke FAST and last known well record”
Use local emergency numbers and pharmacists for medicine questions as a fallback for “Stroke FAST and last known well record”
Use paper handover notes when phones are dead as a fallback for “Stroke FAST and last known well record”
Use a trusted translator if language blocks care as a fallback for “Stroke FAST and last known well record”
failure_signs: Red flags appear: face droop, arm weakness, speech trouble, sudden vision loss, severe headache, or imbalance
The person becomes harder to wake, breathe, drink, speak, walk, or recognize during “Stroke FAST and last known well record”
Pain, bleeding, swelling, fever, confusion, or weakness gets worse during “Stroke FAST and last known well record”
when_not_to_use: Do not delay emergency care to complete the checklist when using the “Stroke FAST and last known well record” record
Do not move a trauma patient unless staying is more dangerous when using the “Stroke FAST and last known well record” record
Do not force care on a conscious person except immediate life safety situations under local rules when using the “Stroke FAST and last known well record” record
short_term: Stabilize the scene, reduce immediate harm, record key facts, and connect the person to trained help. This note applies specifically to Stroke FAST and last known well record.
long_term: Keep medical records updated, train the group in certified first aid, maintain supplies, and practice respectful handovers. This note applies specifically to Stroke FAST and last known well record.
if_method_fails: Escalate to emergency services, nearest clinic, pharmacist, radio medical consult, or evacuation route; document the failed step and time. This note applies specifically to Stroke FAST and last known well record.
environment_notes: In apartments, heat, smoke, poor ventilation, crowds, no elevator, and water outages can make medical problems worse. Plan quiet space, clean water, light, and transport. This note applies specifically to Stroke FAST and last known well record.

title: Seizure protection and aftercare log
category: Medical and first · Aid · Disaster · Health
summary: Medical-heavy survival entry for protecting from injury, timing the seizure, and monitoring recovery. It is designed for offline emergencies and does not replace professional care.
